questions.question: What should happen in the first 5 minutes of a Netflix system design interview?
options: Ask targeted clarifying questions, provide rough capacity numbers (subscribers, concurrent streams, CDN bandwidth), and scope the problem before drawing anything.
Draw the highest-level architecture diagram immediately to show breadth.
Ask the interviewer which subsystem to focus on and then deep-dive only into that.
List all Netflix products and ask which one the question is about.
questions.explanation: Interviewers evaluate your process, not just your output. Starting with clarification and numbers demonstrates engineering calibration and prevents wasted design time on the wrong problem.

questions.question: Which of these is the strongest clarifying question for a 'Design Netflix streaming' prompt?
options: 'Is this VOD only, or does it include live streaming? They have very different latency, CDN fill, and segment-size requirements.'
'How many engineers will be working on this system?'
'What programming language should the backend services use?'
'Should we use AWS or build our own data centers?'
questions.explanation: VOD vs live is a fundamental constraint that changes segment duration, prefill strategy, ABR parameters, and redundancy requirements. It is exactly the kind of clarification that shapes the entire design.

questions.question: When a Netflix interviewer pivots to 'live streaming changes?', what is the most important single change to mention?
options: Segment duration drops to ~2 seconds, proactive prefill is impossible (content is being created in real time), and the origin is a live encoder rather than S3.
Live streaming requires a completely different CDN architecture with no relationship to Open Connect.
The main change is that subscribers need lower-latency devices such as satellite receivers.
Live streaming removes the need for an encoding ladder because all subscribers must receive the same quality.
questions.explanation: Short segments and real-time origin are the defining constraints of live streaming. They cascade into ABR changes, DVR design, and multi-CDN redundancy requirements.

questions.question: A candidate spends 40 of 45 minutes on the encoding pipeline and never discusses delivery or recommendations. What does this signal to the interviewer?
options: The candidate may have deep encoding knowledge but lacks the breadth to design a complete system — a risk for staff-level work where coordination across subsystems is expected.
This is ideal — depth is more valued than breadth at Netflix.
The candidate should be scored higher for demonstrating expert knowledge in one area.
This is acceptable if the encoding pipeline is the hardest part of the system.
questions.explanation: Netflix staff engineers are expected to design across system boundaries. A 45-minute interview that covers one subsystem signals a specialist who may struggle with cross-functional architecture work.

questions.question: How should a candidate respond to an interviewer's deliberate design pivot?
options: Acknowledge the constraint change, briefly state what needs to be updated in the existing design, and adjust without discarding the overall structure.
Start the design over from scratch to show flexibility.
Ask the interviewer to clarify why they are pivoting before answering.
Tell the interviewer that the pivot is out of scope for this design question.
questions.explanation: Pivots test design flexibility and structured thinking. A good candidate adapts the existing design rather than abandoning it. Starting over wastes the 25 minutes of work already done.

questions.question: What does naming a trade-off with a rejected alternative demonstrate?
options: That the candidate considered multiple approaches and made a deliberate choice — not that they only know one pattern and applied it by default.
That the candidate is familiar with multiple cloud providers.
That the candidate has read about the topic but may not have practical experience.
That the design is overly complex with unnecessary alternatives.
questions.explanation: The strongest candidates reason about the option space, not just the chosen option. 'I chose X over Y because Z, at the cost of W' is the trade-off communication pattern Netflix interviewers listen for.

questions.question: At the 45-minute mark, what should a strong-hire candidate's closing look like?
options: A brief summary of the top 2-3 trade-off decisions made and why, what they would instrument first, and one thing they would revisit with more time.
A complete list of every component mentioned during the interview.
A question to the interviewer about what Netflix actually uses in production.
A request to continue the design past the time limit to cover missed areas.
questions.explanation: Interviewers remember the closing disproportionately. A crisp summary that ties trade-offs together shows structured thinking and composure under time pressure.

questions.question: Which score on the 16-point rubric indicates a clear hire recommendation at Netflix?
options: 14-16 points indicates strong hire; 10-13 indicates hire; below 10 indicates mixed or no-hire.
Any score above 8 is sufficient for a hire recommendation at Netflix.
The rubric score does not matter — only the interviewer's subjective impression counts.
A perfect 16 is required for a hire recommendation at Netflix's bar.
questions.explanation: The 16-point rubric in this lesson maps to three tiers. Strong hire requires demonstrating depth, breadth, failure thinking, trade-offs, Netflix vocabulary, and a clean summary.
